event: The Vatican Hill area, called Ager Vaticanus, lay outside the early Roman Republic across the Tiber and was considered an unfavorable, marshy district.
Etruscan and later Roman tradition associated the name Vaticanus with a deity of prophecy whose oracles were given on the hill.
Emperor Caligula built a circus in the Vatican area and erected an Egyptian obelisk later moved to St. Peter's Square.
Tradition holds that the Apostle Peter was martyred in Nero's Circus on the Vatican Hill during the persecution following the Great Fire of Rome.
Emperor Constantine began constructing the original St. Peter's Basilica over the believed burial site of the Apostle Peter.
Pope Vigilius and the city endured the Gothic siege of Rome, underscoring the popes' growing temporal role amid Byzantine wars.
The Lombard threat to Rome prompted the papacy to seek Frankish protection, laying groundwork for an independent papal state.
The Donation of Pepin granted conquered territories to the pope, formally founding the Papal States under papal sovereignty.
Pope Leo III crowned Charlemagne emperor in St. Peter's Basilica, cementing the alliance between the papacy and Frankish power.
Saracen raiders sacked St. Peter's Basilica, which then lay outside Rome's walls.
Pope Leo IV completed the Leonine Wall enclosing the Vatican Hill, creating the fortified Leonine City.
Pope Gregory VII launched the Investiture Controversy with the Holy Roman Empire, asserting papal supremacy over secular rulers.
The papacy relocated to Avignon, beginning a long period during which the Vatican palaces fell into neglect.
Pope Gregory XI returned the papacy to Rome, after which the Vatican gradually became the principal papal residence.
The Western Schism began with rival popes, deeply destabilizing papal authority and the Papal States.
The Council of Constance ended the Western Schism by electing Pope Martin V, restoring a single line of popes in Rome.
Pope Sixtus IV commissioned the Sistine Chapel, which became a center of papal liturgy and Renaissance art.
Pope Julius II laid the cornerstone for the new St. Peter's Basilica, beginning a monumental Renaissance rebuilding.
Michelangelo began painting the Sistine Chapel ceiling under the patronage of Pope Julius II.
The Sack of Rome by mutinous Imperial troops devastated the city and the Vatican, marking a low point for the Renaissance papacy.
Michelangelo completed The Last Judgment on the altar wall of the Sistine Chapel.
Michelangelo was appointed chief architect of St. Peter's and designed its great dome.
Pope Sixtus V had the Vatican obelisk re-erected at the center of St. Peter's Square.
The rebuilt St. Peter's Basilica was consecrated, becoming the largest church in Christendom.
Gian Lorenzo Bernini began designing the great colonnade that frames St. Peter's Square.
French revolutionary forces occupied Rome, proclaimed a Roman Republic, and took Pope Pius VI prisoner.
Napoleon annexed the Papal States to the French Empire and arrested Pope Pius VII.
The Papal States were restored to the pope following Napoleon's fall and the Congress of Vienna settlement.
Revolutionaries proclaimed the short-lived Roman Republic, forcing Pope Pius IX to flee before French troops restored him.
Italian forces captured Rome, ending the Papal States and confining the pope to the Vatican as a self-declared prisoner.
Italy's Law of Guarantees offered the pope limited privileges, which the papacy rejected, beginning the Roman Question standoff.
The Lateran Treaty with Italy created the sovereign Vatican City State, resolving the Roman Question.
Vatican Radio was inaugurated, giving the Holy See an independent global broadcasting voice.
Pope Pius XII began his pontificate on the eve of World War II, steering Vatican neutrality amid intense controversy.
The Holy See condemned communism, decreeing excommunication for Catholics who actively supported communist parties.
Pope John XXIII opened the Second Vatican Council, launching sweeping reforms of the Catholic Church.
The Second Vatican Council concluded under Pope Paul VI, reshaping liturgy, ecumenism, and the Church's relations with the modern world.
Karol Wojtyla became Pope John Paul II, the first non-Italian pope in over four centuries.
Pope John Paul II survived an assassination attempt by gunfire in St. Peter's Square.
A revised concordat with Italy ended Roman Catholicism's status as Italy's official state religion.
Vatican City introduced its own constitution-era institutions and the Holy See modernized its financial and diplomatic structures.
The Vatican hosted the Great Jubilee, drawing millions of pilgrims to Rome for the new millennium.
Pope John Paul II died and was succeeded by Pope Benedict XVI.
Benedict XVI became the first pope in nearly 600 years to resign, and Jorge Mario Bergoglio was elected Pope Francis.
Pope Francis canonized John XXIII and John Paul II in a landmark joint ceremony in St. Peter's Square.
Pope Francis hosted an unprecedented Vatican summit on the protection of minors to confront clergy sexual abuse.
The COVID-19 pandemic led Pope Francis to deliver an extraordinary blessing to an empty St. Peter's Square.
Pope Francis died and the conclave elected Robert Prevost as Pope Leo XIV, the first U.S.-born pope.
